highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.187: Sandbox Credential Protection & Org Model Restrictions
highlights.summary: The latest release adds a `sandbox.credentials` setting that prevents sandboxed commands from accessing credential files and secret environment variables, improving security for team workflows. Organizations can now restrict which models users are allowed to select via the model picker, `--model` flag, or `ANTHROPIC_MODEL` environment variable. If you're using Claude Code in a team or enterprise setting, check with your admin about any new model restrictions that may apply to you.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.186: CLI MCP Login & Workflow Status Filtering
highlights.summary: You can now authenticate MCP servers directly from the command line using `claude mcp login <name>` and `claude mcp logout <name>`, without needing to open the interactive `/mcp` menu — particularly useful when working over SSH with `--no-browser`. The `/workflows` agent view also gains a status filter (press `f`) to help you quickly find workflows by their current state.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.183: Auto Mode Now Blocks Destructive Git Commands
highlights.summary: Claude Code's auto mode has been made safer by blocking destructive git operations like `git reset --hard`, `git clean -fd`, and `git stash drop` when you haven't explicitly asked to discard local work. It also prevents `git commit --amend` on commits not made by the agent in the current session, protecting your git history from accidental changes. This is a great quality-of-life improvement for beginners who run Claude Code in auto mode and worry about losing uncommitted work.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.181: Set Config From the Prompt with `/config key=value`
highlights.summary: A handy new `/config key=value` syntax lets you change any Claude Code setting directly from the prompt without editing config files — for example, `/config thinking=false` instantly toggles extended thinking off. This works in interactive mode, with `-p` for non-interactive runs, and in Remote Control sessions. Beginners will find this much easier than hunting through settings files to make quick adjustments.

highlights.title: Show HN: Recall – Local Project Memory for Claude Code
highlights.summary: Recall is an open-source tool that gives Claude Code persistent, local memory about your projects, so it can remember context across sessions without you having to re-explain your codebase each time. This addresses one of the most common pain points for developers using Claude Code on long-running projects. If you find yourself repeating project context at the start of every session, Recall is worth trying.

highlights.title: Agent Rigor – Stop Your AI Coding Assistant from Doom-Looping
highlights.summary: Agent Rigor is a community tool designed to detect and interrupt the "doom-loop" pattern where AI coding agents get stuck repeatedly attempting the same failing action. This is a real problem when running Claude Code in auto mode on complex tasks, and can waste significant API credits. The tool adds guardrails to break the loop and surface the failure for human review.

highlights.title: From Zero to Claude Code in 19 Minutes (No Code)
highlights.summary: This beginner-focused YouTube tutorial walks you through getting started with Claude Code from scratch in under 20 minutes, requiring no prior coding knowledge. It's a great first watch if you've just heard about Claude Code and want to see what it can actually do before diving into the documentation. Pair it with the official VS Code integration for the smoothest onboarding experience.

highlights.title: Mastering Claude Code in 30 Minutes
highlights.summary: A concise 30-minute YouTube guide aimed at developers who want to move beyond the basics and get more productive with Claude Code quickly. The format is ideal for intermediate users who already have Claude Code installed and want to learn power-user workflows without sitting through a multi-hour course. Watch it during a lunch break to immediately level up your daily Claude Code usage.

tips_of_the_day: Use `/config thinking=false` at the start of quick, simple tasks to speed up responses and reduce token usage — save extended thinking for genuinely complex reasoning challenges.

If you run Claude Code in auto mode, make sure you're on v2.1.183 or later so destructive git commands are blocked by default. As an extra safety net, always work on a feature branch rather than directly on `main` when letting the agent run freely.

When authenticating a new MCP server over an SSH connection, use `claude mcp login <name> --no-browser` to complete the auth flow via stdin redirect instead of struggling to open a browser on a headless remote machine.
